title_en: Introduction to Nihayat al-Muhtaj and ar-Ramli
title_ar: مقدمة في نهاية المحتاج ومؤلفه الرملي
content_en:
Nihayat al-Muhtaj ila Sharh al-Minhaj (The Ultimate Guide: Commentary on the Minhaj) is one of the two great authoritative commentaries on an-Nawawi's Minhaj al-Talibin, the other being Tuhfat al-Muhtaj by Ibn Hajar al-Haytami. Together, these two works define the later Shafi'i school in the Arab world (particularly Egypt and the Levant for Nihayat al-Muhtaj, and the Hijaz and Southeast Asia for Tuhfat al-Muhtaj), and any serious question in Shafi'i law is typically resolved by consulting both.

The author, Shams ad-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad ar-Ramli al-Ansari (919–1004 AH / 1513–1596 CE), was one of the most distinguished Shafi'i scholars of the Ottoman period. Known as 'al-Shafi'i as-Saghir' (the Little Shafi'i) for the breadth and depth of his command of the school, ar-Ramli represents the Egyptian tradition of Shafi'i scholarship in its mature form. He studied under the greatest scholars of his era in Cairo and became a leading teacher at Al-Azhar, where his opinions shaped Shafi'i practice for generations.

Ar-Ramli's authority is based not merely on Nihayat al-Muhtaj but on a lifetime of fatawa (legal opinions) that his students and contemporaries preserved. When ar-Ramli and Ibn Hajar al-Haytami — the two titans of the later Shafi'i school — agree, their combined opinion is definitive for the school. When they differ, scholars in Egypt and the Levant tend to follow ar-Ramli, while those in the Hijaz and Southeast Asia (particularly Indonesia and Malaysia) tend to follow Ibn Hajar. Both positions are within the recognized Shafi'i tradition.

Nihayat al-Muhtaj was begun by ar-Ramli's father (also a Shafi'i scholar) and completed by the son, who substantially rewrote and expanded it to the comprehensive work we have today. The commentary closely follows the Minhaj's text, explaining each phrase and position, supplying the evidential basis from the Quran and Sunnah, noting differences between ar-Rafi'i and an-Nawawi, and giving ar-Ramli's own analysis of which view is stronger or more reliable.

A key feature of Nihayat al-Muhtaj is its attention to practical fatwa. Where Mughni al-Muhtaj (ash-Shirbini's commentary) is somewhat more theoretical and formal, ar-Ramli frequently adds practical guidance on what a person should actually do given the legal analysis — making the work especially useful for muftis and judges. His frequent phrases like 'wa lil-mufti al-ifta' bihi' (the mufti may give a fatwa based on this) indicate his concern for practical legal guidance.

The work also reflects ar-Ramli's broad knowledge of hadith, which he brings to bear in resolving disputed questions within the Shafi'i school. He is careful to distinguish between the stronger and weaker views within the school (al-asahh vs. the other), to note when an opinion attributed to an-Nawawi is actually not found in his authenticated works, and to point out cases where the school's later authorities have departed from an-Nawawi on the basis of stronger evidence.

For students and scholars of Shafi'i jurisprudence, Nihayat al-Muhtaj stands alongside Tuhfat al-Muhtaj as an essential reference — comprehensive, authoritative, and still actively consulted by Shafi'i scholars and muftis in the Arab world and Southeast Asia today.
